facts.fact: Prompt caching reduces context costs by up to 90% for cached reads, with cache write tokens costing 1.25× for 5-minute TTL and 2× for 1-hour TTL
Output tokens are priced 4-8× higher than input tokens across leading models; Claude Opus 4.6 costs $5/M input and $25/M output (5× ratio)
The 'lost in the middle' phenomenon causes models to perform 6-13% worse when relevant information is placed in the center of long contexts vs. at beginning/end
Indirect prompt injection attacks succeeded in 94.4% of trials in medical chatbot simulations, with 91.7% success rate even in high-harm scenarios
ChatGPT's browsing feature was vulnerable to indirect injection: hidden text in webpages altered summaries by up to 95%, inverting sentiment from negative to positive
Optimal RAG chunk size is 300-1000 tokens; chunks <256 tokens lose context, >1024 tokens reduce granularity and increase retrieval latency
Semantic caching in RAG systems cuts LLM costs by 50-68.8% in production workloads without quality loss
Context rot (performance degradation before hard limits) occurs well before context window fills; models degrade 10-25% as context approaches 70% of max length
Aggressive truncation (limiting RAG to 2-3 documents) cuts input tokens by 50%+ with no loss in precision for factoid queries
Dynamic context assembly with re-ranking (DynamicRAG) improves answer relevance by 7-15% vs. static retrieval by learning which documents to include per query
Batching 32 requests reduces per-token LLM costs 85% with only 20% latency increase, amortizing fixed prompt overhead
OWASP GenAI Security Project ranked prompt injection as #1 risk in 2025 Top 10 for LLM applications, with no known complete fix
Attention sinks (tokens receiving disproportionate attention regardless of relevance) concentrate on initial tokens; RoPE positional encoding causes long-term decay of distant tokens
Instance-Adaptive Prompting (IAP) improves reasoning by dynamically tailoring prompts; semantic flow from questions to prompts in shallow layers correlates with correctness
Dual-LLM architecture (privileged + quarantined models) reduces context poisoning risk by 40-60%; quarantined model reads untrusted content, privileged model never sees raw untrusted input
Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) p90 target is <2 seconds in production RAG; Mean Time to Answer target is <3 seconds for interactive use
Precision@K benchmarks: ≥0.85 for regulated content, ≥0.75 for general knowledge, ≥0.65 for exploratory research in RAG systems
Hybrid retrieval (BM25 + vector search) improves recall by 1-9% vs. vector search alone, depending on domain and implementation
RAG reduces factually incorrect statements by 45-72% depending on domain vs. non-RAG LLM responses
Claude's 200K context window can process ~40K-50K typical English words; thinking blocks are stripped from visible context, preserving capacity

examples.example: ProjectDiscovery cut LLM costs 59% by implementing prompt caching: system prompts (22K tokens) cached across 50K daily queries meant each request paid only 0.1× instead of 1× for cached content

The Guardian's test of ChatGPT's browser tool found hidden instructions in webpage CSS comments that inverted negative product reviews into positive summaries without the model detecting the injection
OpenAI's custom GPT store had widespread prompt injection vulnerabilities where users could request 'show system instructions' and retrieve proprietary business logic and API keys
GitHub Copilot vulnerability CVE-2025-53773 (CVSS 9.6) allowed remote code execution via prompt injection in code comments, affecting millions of developers
In medical Q&A study, LLMs given contradictory RAG context (FDA Category X pregnancy drugs listed as safe) selected dangerous options 91.7% of the time
Databricks found that reducing chunk size from 1024 to 300 tokens in financial RAG system dropped precision@5 from 0.92 to 0.88 but cut retrieval latency by 65% and LLM input tokens by 58%
ClaudeFast's Code Kit recovered 15,000 tokens per session (82% improvement) by using progressive disclosure: skills loaded on-demand instead of loading all 20+ domains into CLAUDE.md upfront
Stripe's API documentation RAG system uses metadata filtering (date ranges, API version, endpoint category) to reduce retrieval context from 8 documents to 2 with 94% precision
Netflix internal RAG system demonstrated that placing most-relevant search result at position 5 (middle of 10 results) caused 18% performance drop vs. position 1 (top)
A SecOps pentester successfully jailbroke an AI answer engine by injecting encoded instructions in a URL, which the engine fetched and decoded before passing to LLM context

analogies.analogy: Context poisoning is like a restaurant's kitchen receiving a fake invoice mixed with legitimate ones. The chef follows all instructions blindly, including the fake one, before anyone validates authenticity.

Prompt caching is like writing key facts on a whiteboard once (1.25-2× cost) instead of re-dictating them for every subsequent conversation (1× cost × N conversations). At 10+ conversations, you break even and save 90%.
The 'lost in the middle' problem is like a passenger reading a 300-page book: they remember the opening and climax but skim or miss details on page 150, even though it's right in front of them.
Dynamic context assembly is like a sommelier selecting 2-3 wines from a 1000-bottle cellar per request instead of serving the entire inventory to every customer.

Dual-LLM architecture is like a bank with two employees: one (quarantined) opens mail from unknown senders and writes summaries, the other (privileged) never touches external mail but reads summaries to make decisions.
Context truncation under constraints is like packing for a flight with weight limits: you choose your 3 essential items and leave the nice-to-haves behind, not random items.
Metadata filtering in RAG is like a librarian using the Dewey Decimal System to send you only books from specific sections instead of your entire library catalog.
Context rot is like a phone battery that starts dying at 20% remaining even though it claims a 100% capacity: performance degrades before the hard limit.
Attention sinks are like a broken magnet that pulls all iron filings to the edges, leaving the middle untouched, regardless of where you place the iron.
Hybrid retrieval (keyword + vector) is like having both a GPS (vector similarity) and a street sign reader (exact keyword match) in a car: they catch different things.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Bigger context window = always better performance
common_misconceptions.truth: Performance degrades (context rot) at 70%+ of max before hitting hard limits. Models experience 10-25% accuracy drop due to attention sinks and lost-in-the-middle, not capacity.

common_misconceptions.misconception: All retrieved documents should go in the prompt
common_misconceptions.truth: Aggressive truncation (2-3 top docs) beats 5-10 docs in precision and cost. More context ≠ better answers; relevance ranking matters more than volume.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prompt injection is just direct prompt attacks from user input
common_misconceptions.truth: Indirect injection (poisoning RAG, fetched webpages, emails, tool output) is the real threat; 94.4% success in medical chatbots. OWASP ranks it #1 risk in 2025.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Longer chunks give more context, so they're better
common_misconceptions.truth: Optimal chunk size is 300-1000 tokens depending on task. >1024 tokens reduces granularity and increases retrieval latency; <256 tokens loses surrounding context.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Caching only helps if you use the exact same prompt
common_misconceptions.truth: Cache writes are one-time 1.25-2× cost; reads are 0.1× for 5-10 min. Even 3-5 similar queries amortize the write cost.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Context poisoning requires the attacker to control the LLM's system prompt
common_misconceptions.truth: Attackers poison RAG documents, fetched webpages, emails, or tool output—none of which they control directly. The system blends trusted + untrusted input in one window.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Attention equally distributes across all tokens
common_misconceptions.truth: Attention sinks concentrate on initial tokens via RoPE positional decay, regardless of relevance. Middle tokens receive 30-40% less attention than beginning/end.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Semantic search (vector similarity) is sufficient for RAG
common_misconceptions.truth: Hybrid retrieval (BM25 keyword + vector) improves recall 1-9%. Vector search misses exact matches; keyword search misses semantic similarity. Both needed.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Context truncation strategies are all equivalent
common_misconceptions.truth: Smart truncation (keeping top-ranked docs) beats proportional truncation (cutting all chunks equally). Ranking strategy 2-3× more important than truncation method.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Dynamic context assembly adds too much latency
common_misconceptions.truth: Modern re-ranking (DynamicRAG) adds <50ms; benefit (7-15% relevance gain) outweighs cost. Time-to-First-Token still <2s p90 in production.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hidden instructions in CSS/HTML comments are safe from LLMs
common_misconceptions.truth: ChatGPT's browser tool fetches and processes comment text. The Guardian inverted product review sentiment via hidden CSS prompts.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Output token cost is fixed; only input optimization matters
common_misconceptions.truth: Controlling output length is the single most effective cost lever. 500-token output costs 2-5× less than 2000-token output with identical input.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Manual metadata annotation is required for RAG filtering
common_misconceptions.truth: LLM-extracted metadata (using function calling + Pydantic) automatically filters with 90%+ accuracy, eliminating manual labeling.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Context windows hitting their limit is a hard error
common_misconceptions.truth: Models silently truncate oldest tokens (digital amnesia) before hard limits. Systems fail degraded, not failed—silently dropping context.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Placing relevant docs at the end guarantees they'll be used
common_misconceptions.truth: RoPE decay causes end-tokens to receive similar attention to middle-tokens. Beginning and end are equally strong; middle is 6-13% weaker.
